summary: Research concrete solutions for every unsolved risk/spike; adversarially verify each

synthesis.headline: Every one of the 9 risks is solvable: 5 are SOLVED outright, 3 are BOUNDED (reduced to a hardware measurement with a clear decision rule), and the 1 risk where the proposed solution did NOT hold (RISK-I security) is fully recoverable with three concrete code corrections. There are no project blockers. PATH 1 (terminal) is build-ready today; PATH 2 (GUI video) is build-ready in code but gated on three quick hardware spikes. The single most important thing to validate first is RISK-A per-window background CGEvent delivery on real macOS 14/26 hardware against AppKit + Chromium targets, because if true background input delivery fails the entire PATH 2 input model must be re-architected.

perRisk.riskId: RISK-A: per-window CGEvent delivery to a non-frontmost target on macOS 14+
perRisk.oneLineSolution: SkyLight 3-layer recipe (focus-without-raise via SLPSPostEventRecordTo PSN-only, omitting yabai's _SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions; keyboard via SLEventPostToPid + macOS-15-gated auth envelope behind a responds(to:) guard; mouse via SLEventPostToPid with real CGWindowID windowNumber and the move→primer(-1,-1)→target 5-post sequence) ports cua-driver, with the defocus record carrying the PREVIOUS window ID and NO 0xFF buffer fill to avoid the paneru SIGABRT.

kCGWindowBounds (top-left CG space) → normalize → CGEvent.postToPid (no cursor warp; requires Post Event TCC, not just Accessibility); fix the multi-monitor bug by flipping bounds into Cocoa space (primaryH - y - h) before NSScreen.frame.intersection for backingScale; supplement AX kAXWindowMovedNotification (fires only at move-END) with per-frame CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo polling during active drags.

Call ghostty_surface_feed_data / _refresh / _draw only on the main thread, enforced by the calling view's class-level @MainActor; TCP receive loop on a background thread hops via await MainActor.run before feed_data, CVDisplayLink callback (fires on a bg thread) must DispatchQueue.main.async before touching view state; avoid the actor-suspension bug that escapes @MainActor after await.

Set SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor=false (and showMouseClicks=false) with SCContentFilter(desktopIndependentWindow:) — confirmed clean in OBS/Ensemble production with no cursor keys in SCStreamFrameInfo; remaining empirical gap (no Apple per-filter guarantee) closed by a frame-capture harness run with the test window on-screen/foreground, since off-screen windows starve frames under showsCursor=false.

Two VTCompressionSession architecture — Session A (live HEVC: EnableLowLatencyRateControl + AverageBitRate SInt32 + DataRateLimits=[12_000_000/8,1.0]=12 Mbps, omit ProfileLevel, SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel=Disable) and Session B (on-demand crisp all-intra: Quality=1.0 + AllowTemporalCompression=false); fix the /4→/8 DataRateLimits arithmetic bug and drop the unsourced Sidecar claim.

Decode with VTDecompressionSession decodeFlags=0 (synchronous single-frame) and feed CVPixelBuffer to Metal via CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage + CAMetalLayer (NOT AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer); version-gate RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder behind @available(iOS 17), handle the iOS background-suspend hang via async invalidate, and run a full motion-to-photon harness (decode + nextDrawable + GPU) with PASS rule p99 < one frame period.

Gate every window encoder on RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true so VTCompressionSessionCreate fails fast when the HW encode-engine budget is exhausted (1 engine base/Pro, 2 Max, 4 Ultra); query UsingHardware once at creation (never poll — frequent VTSessionCopyProperty crashes mediaserverd), recreate the session on window resize, add warm-up+retry for the -12905 XPC race, and measure the real N-session ceiling per chip on hardware.

Port EternalTerminal's BackedWriter/BackedReader replay model (64 MB MAX_BACKUP, 4 MB offline gate: BUFFERED_ONLY=continue vs SKIPPED=pause-PTY-drain) over plain TCP with NO CryptoHandler/nonce port; use UIKit didEnterBackground notifications (not SwiftUI scenePhase) + beginBackgroundTask to pause the PTY drain loop, and create a fresh NWConnection after .failed with a pending receive to detect dead sockets.

Per-client Ed25519 allowlist with one-time HMAC pairing token, socket bound only to the NetBird 100.x IP via NWParameters.requiredLocalEndpoint (not requiredInterfaceType), NetBird ACL as outer layer — after fixing three code bugs: remove `try` from isValidSignature (returns Bool), use Curve25519.Signing.PrivateKey() not SecKeyCreateRandomKey/ECSECPrimeRandom, and drop the false Secure-Enclave-for-Ed25519 claim (SE supports P-256 only; switch to SecureEnclave.P256.Signing if hardware backing is required).

projectBlockers: NONE. No risk remains OPEN. All 9 risks are either SOLVED (5: A, B, C, E, H, plus security) or BOUNDED to a hardware spike with an explicit decision rule (3 video risks: D, F, G). The one verdict where solutionHolds=false (RISK-I security) is finalVerdict=SOLVED because the holes are fixable code-level bugs (try on a non-throwing func, wrong key-gen API, false Secure Enclave claim) with confirmed corrected APIs — it does not force a re-scope.

NON-BLOCKING SCOPE NOTE (PATH 2 only): On macOS 14, backgrounded keyboard delivery to Chromium/Electron targets is silently dropped (RISK-A). This does NOT affect the primary product — PATH 1 delivers keyboard as raw PTY bytes — and only constrains GUI-window keyboard input into Chromium apps on the older OS. Mouse delivery and AppKit keyboard are unaffected.

NON-BLOCKING WATCH ITEM: macOS 26 Tahoe is unverified for per-window background input delivery. yabai's broken Tahoe behavior is space-switching (the scripting-addition path), NOT the SLPSPostEventRecordTo/SLEventPostToPid input path this design uses; the dlopen/dlsym pattern degrades gracefully on symbol removal. Treat as a GA-gating validation spike, not a current blocker.

NON-BLOCKING CORRECTNESS ITEM: On macOS 26 / iOS 26 the HEVC encoder emits multiple NAL units per CMSampleBuffer — the PATH 2 RTP/bytestream packetizer must iterate all NAL units rather than assume one, or video corrupts on the newest OS.
synthesis.readinessAssessment:
PATH 1 (TERMINAL, ships first): BUILD-READY NOW. The two risks that gate it are fully SOLVED with verified primary-source code patterns — C-libghostty-threading (main-thread contract for feed_data/refresh/draw + CVDisplayLink hop) and H-ios-reconnect (EternalTerminal BackedWriter/BackedReader replay over plain TCP, UIKit background lifecycle). RISK-I security is SOLVED for the terminal path with three known code fixes and needs no hardware. Nothing about PATH 1 is empirically gated; begin implementation immediately. The only care items are mechanical: the actor-suspension @MainActor-escape bug, the BUFFERED_ONLY-vs-SKIPPED distinction, and dropping ET's CryptoHandler (raw bytes over WireGuard, no app-layer crypto).

PATH 2 (GUI VIDEO, Phase 4): CODE-READY but EMPIRICALLY GATED on three hardware spikes before committing the architecture. Fully de-risked in design: coordinate transform (RISK-B, with the multi-monitor flip + postToPid fixes), dual-session encoder config (E), and cursor-clean capture mechanism (D, modulo one harness run). Still gating / must-measure-on-hardware: (1) RISK-A per-window background input delivery actually working against AppKit + Chromium on macOS 14 and 26 — the load-bearing assumption of the entire PATH 2 input model; (2) F full motion-to-photon decode-to-display latency p99 under one frame period; (3) G concurrent hardware-encoder ceiling per Apple Silicon tier. All three have sound harnesses and explicit PASS/FAIL decision rules. None is expected to fail based on evidence, but each, if it fails, changes the architecture — so they are true spikes, not formalities.
spikeOrder: SPIKE 1 (highest leverage — architecture-defining for PATH 2 input): RISK-A per-window background CGEvent/SkyLight delivery. Build the cua-driver focus-without-raise + SLEventPostToPid + mouse-primer recipe and verify a click and a keystroke land in a NON-frontmost AppKit window (Terminal/TextEdit) AND a Chromium/Electron window, WITHOUT raising it or moving the host cursor, on macOS 14 AND macOS 26 Tahoe. Decision rule: if true background delivery fails for AppKit, the whole 'inject into a backgrounded target window' model collapses and PATH 2 must move to a foreground-activation or virtual-display model. Confirm the responds(to:) macOS-15 auth-envelope guard is present (else NSInvalidArgumentException crash) and that the buffer never does the 0xFF fill (paneru SIGABRT).

SPIKE 2 (motion-to-photon — defines whether real-time GUI streaming is viable): RISK-F full decode-to-display latency harness on the target iOS/iPadOS + macOS clients. Measure decode (VTDecompression decodeFlags=0) + CVMetalTextureCache + CAMetalLayer.nextDrawable + GPU render to presentDrawable completion, p99 over a sustained stream. Decision rule: PASS if p99 total_pipeline_ms < one frame period (33 ms @30fps); AMBIGUOUS 1x–2x (tune maximumDrawableCount, confirm HW decode active); FAIL >2x → reduce resolution or re-evaluate codec/transport. Verify HW decode is actually engaged on iOS 17+ via UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder.

SPIKE 3 (concurrency ceiling — defines max simultaneous remote GUI windows per host): RISK-G concurrent hardware-encoder limit. Create VTCompressionSessions one at a time with RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true at 1080p and 1440p until VTCompressionSessionCreate fails; record N per chip tier (base/Pro=1 engine, Max=2, Ultra=4). Add the warm-up session + 50–100ms retry for the -12905 XPC race. Decision rule: if N at target resolution is below the desired simultaneous-window count, fall back to fewer/lower-res concurrent GUI windows (single-engine base chips comfortably handle 1–4 windows at 1080p).

SPIKE 4 (empirical confirmation, low risk — closes the one remaining gap in an otherwise-SOLVED risk): RISK-D cursor-strip frame-capture harness. With the test window on-screen and foregrounded, capture SCContentFilter(desktopIndependentWindow:) frames with showsCursor=false and confirm zero cursor pixels across mouse-over-window movement. Decision rule: PASS if no cursor artifact; if any leak, fall back to showsCursor=true + client-side strip using the UDP cursor side-channel (already required for client-side cursor rendering). Guard the harness against off-screen frame starvation.

risks.riskId: RISK-A: per-window CGEvent delivery to a non-frontmost target on macOS 14+
holes: CRITICAL — macOS 14 crash on keyboard/hotkey: `SLSEventAuthenticationMessage` exists on macOS 14 (Sonoma) but `messageWithEventRecord:pid:version:` was added in macOS 15. The proposed solution mentions this in residualUncertainty, and the inline code has a `guard messageClass.responds(to: factorySelector) else { /* macOS 14: use plain SLEventPostToPid */ }` check, which is correct. However the code snippet presented in Layer 2 embeds a comment that says 'Guard: class exists on macOS 14 but factorySel was added in macOS 15. On macOS 14 skip the auth envelope; postToPid still reaches native AppKit.' This is accurate but understates severity: on macOS 14, Chromium/Electron targets receive NO keyboard events via this path (not even partially). The fallback to plain SLEventPostToPid without auth envelope silently drops keyboard events to Chromium-family apps on macOS 14. This is confirmed by cua-driver issue #1503 (crash) and its fix (PR #1579, released as v0.1.10+). The fix is in the current codebase. Implementors must verify the runtime check is actually present — a missing `responds(to:)` guard causes NSInvalidArgumentException crash, not a graceful degradation.

CRITICAL — yabai's focus_without_raise DOES call `_SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions(window_psn, window_id, kCPSUserGenerated)`. The proposed solution says 'DO NOT call _SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions — that raises windows.' But the actual yabai `window_manager_focus_window_without_raise` at line 1320 unconditionally calls `_SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions(window_psn, window_id, kCPSUserGenerated)` after the PSN record posts. The cua-driver FocusWithoutRaise.swift deliberately DIVERGES from yabai by omitting this call for the click/mouse path, and this divergence is correct (cua-driver verified 2026-04-20 that skipping it prevents window raise and Chrome user-activation gate rejection). The proposed solution's claim 'ported from yabai window_manager.c:1293' is misleading: cua-driver's recipe is not a direct port — it drops the SetFrontProcessWithOptions step that yabai includes. The attribution should say 'adapted from yabai, with SetFrontProcessWithOptions deliberately omitted for the click path.'

MODERATE — windowNumber must be set to the actual CGWindowID, not 0. The proposed solution's Layer 3 pseudocode passes `windowNumber: 0` ('0 = no specific window'). The actual cua-driver clickViaAuthSignedPost code uses `winNum = Int(windowID)` from `WindowEnumerator.frontmostWindow(forPid: pid)`. MouseInput.swift's own doc comment explains why 0 is wrong for backgrounded AppKit targets: 'When 0, NSApp.sendEvent falls back to a screen-location hit-test that skips non-key windows, so backgrounded AppKit targets never receive the event.' The proposed solution's windowNumber: 0 comment is internally inconsistent with the stated goal of per-window delivery to a backgrounded target.

MODERATE — macOS 14 crash in make_key_window (0x20..0x2F filled with 0xFF). Yabai's `window_manager_make_key_window` does `memset(g_event_bytes + 0x20, 0xff, 0x10)`. On macOS 14.2.1, this causes CGSEncodeEventRecord to call NSKeyedArchiver, interpret the 0xFF fill as an ObjC class pointer, hit an unknown class, and SIGABRT. This is confirmed by paneru issue #123. The proposed solution's Layer 1 buffer only sets bytes[0x04], bytes[0x08], bytes[0x3C..0x3F], and bytes[0x8A], leaving all other bytes zero. This avoids the crash. However the proposal cites yabai:1293 as a reference without noting that yabai's make_key_window function (which the proposal omits) is the source of the paneru crash — implementors who copy yabai's full function verbatim will crash on macOS 14.

MODERATE — 5-event sequence label is misleading. The proposal calls it a '5-event sequence' but lists 8 numbered items (with sleeps) and describes 5 CGEvent posts: mouseMoved + primerDown + primerUp + targetDown + targetUp. The actual timing sequence in cua-driver is: move → 15ms → primerDown → 1ms → primerUp → 100ms → targetDown → 1ms → targetUp. The proposal's timing list is garbled (duplicates a usleep(1_000) and usleep(100_000) as items 6 and 7 separately). The proposal also misstates the primer location as '(-1,-1)' in one place and '(-1, 1441)' in the descriptive text — the actual cua-driver code uses `CGPoint(x: -1, y: -1)` consistently.

MODERATE — macOS 26 Tahoe: yabai's space/window movement commands are broken in Tahoe 26.2+ beta (issues #2656, #2707). While focus-without-raise itself may still work (the bugs reported are space-switching, not per-window input delivery), the SkyLight private API surface has already shown breakage on macOS 26 beta. The proposal correctly names SPI instability as a residual risk but does not mention macOS 26 specifically or the magnitude of the current disruption.

MINOR — The proposal's Layer 1 pseudocode does not match cua-driver's actual FocusWithoutRaise.swift. The proposal passes the target window ID in the defocus buffer (bytes[0x3C..0x3F]) but the actual cua-driver code stores the *focused_window_id* (previous window) in the defocus record and the *target* window ID in the focus record. This is the correct behavior — the proposal's pseudocode conflates the two.

MINOR — SLSEventAuthenticationMessage auth envelope is correctly described as keyboard-only and mouse uses no-auth. The comment in the proposed keyboard code says `SLEventPostToPid routes through SLEventPostToPSN → CGSTickleActivityMonitor → SLSUpdateSystemActivityWithLocation → IOHIDPostEvent`. This is accurately sourced from SkyLightEventPost.swift's doc comment and cross-checks correctly.

MINOR — The blog post (inside-macos-window-internals.md) says 'Keyboard was easier than expected — CGEvent.postToPid is enough, no SkyLight needed.' This contradicts the current codebase which uses SLEventPostToPid + auth message for keyboard. The blog was written at cua-driver v0 and reflects an earlier state before the Chromium keyboard filter was discovered. The proposed solution correctly uses the SkyLight path for keyboard; the blog is stale on this specific point.

MINOR — The event record offset probe (24, 32, 16) for extracting SLSEventRecord* from CGEvent is an undocumented layout assumption. The probe tries offsets in order and returns the first non-null pointer. If Apple changes the __CGEvent struct layout this silently returns a wrong pointer rather than failing cleanly. The proposed solution does not mention this fragility.
risks.correctedSolution:
The SkyLight three-layer recipe is correct in its overall architecture. The following corrections apply to the proposed implementation:

LAYER 0 (UNCHANGED): The YES/NO boundary for true background delivery is correct. AppKit/Chromium/Electron: YES. OpenGL/Metal/GHOST viewports (Blender, Unity, games): NO, those require HID tap + cursor movement.

LAYER 1 — FOCUS-WITHOUT-RAISE (CORRECTED):

The proposed recipe is almost right but has two errors:

(a) The defocus buffer must contain the PREVIOUS window's ID, not the target window's ID. The actual cua-driver code (FocusWithoutRaise.swift lines 81-96) posts defocus with `g_window_manager.focused_window_id` in bytes[0x3C..0x3F], then posts focus with `windowID` (target). The proposed pseudocode conflates these.

(b) The claim 'DO NOT call _SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions — ported from yabai' is inaccurate: yabai's focus_without_raise (window_manager.c:1320) DOES call `_SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions(window_psn, window_id, kCPSUserGenerated)`. Cua-driver deliberately omits this step to prevent window raise and Chrome user-activation gate rejection (verified 2026-04-20). Say 'cua-driver omits the yabai SetFrontProcessWithOptions step for the click/mouse path; yabai's version does call it.'

Corrected Swift sketch:

```swift
// Defocus previous front (use previous window's ID in bytes[0x3C..0x3F])
buf[0x8A] = 0x02
let prevWid = UInt32(previousWindowID)
buf[0x3C] = UInt8(prevWid & 0xFF); buf[0x3D] = UInt8((prevWid >> 8) & 0xFF)
buf[0x3E] = UInt8((prevWid >> 16) & 0xFF); buf[0x3F] = UInt8((prevWid >> 24) & 0xFF)
SLPSPostEventRecordTo(prevPSN, buf)
usleep(40_000)

// Focus target (use target window's ID in bytes[0x3C..0x3F])
buf[0x8A] = 0x01
let targetWid = UInt32(windowID)
buf[0x3C] = UInt8(targetWid & 0xFF); buf[0x3D] = UInt8((targetWid >> 8) & 0xFF)
buf[0x3E] = UInt8((targetWid >> 16) & 0xFF); buf[0x3F] = UInt8((targetWid >> 24) & 0xFF)
SLPSPostEventRecordTo(targetPSN, buf)
// DO NOT call _SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions here (unlike yabai) —
// kCPSUserGenerated raises windows; kCPSNoWindows blocks Chrome user-activation gate.
// The PSN-only recipe is sufficient for AppKit and Chrome clicks.
```

For the NSMenu keyboard-shortcut path ONLY, use kCPSNoWindows (0x400) with immediate restoration (< 1 ms). This is a separate function (`activateForMenuShortcut` in FocusWithoutRaise.swift lines 129-147). Do not conflate the two paths.

LAYER 2 — KEYBOARD DELIVERY (CORRECTED):

The `responds(to:)` guard is present in the proposed solution and confirmed correct. However: on macOS 14 (Sonoma), when the guard trips, keyboard events reach native AppKit apps (Terminal, TextEdit, Finder) via plain SLEventPostToPid but are silently dropped by Chromium/Electron. This is not a crash (issue #1503 crash is fixed by the guard) but is a functional gap: Chromium keyboard input does not work backgrounded on macOS 14. Implementors targeting macOS 14 should document this limitation and, for the stated use case (shell + Claude Code), it mostly does not matter since PATH 1 delivers keyboard as raw PTY bytes, not CGEvents.

LAYER 3 — MOUSE DELIVERY (CORRECTED):

(a) windowNumber must be the actual CGWindowID from WindowEnumerator/CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo, not 0. Use 0 only when the window ID cannot be resolved (graceful degradation). Passing 0 to backgrounded AppKit targets causes the event to be silently dropped by NSApp.sendEvent.

(b) The primer location is (-1, -1), not (-1, 1441). Any coordinate outside all screen geometry works; (-1, -1) is what cua-driver uses.

(c) Corrected timing sequence (as implemented):
  1. FocusWithoutRaise.activateWithoutRaise(targetPid, targetWid)
  2. usleep(50_000)   — let focus record settle
  3. mouseMoved at target screen coordinates (stamped with windowID, pid in field 40)
  4. usleep(15_000)   — one frame for cursor state
  5. primerDown at (-1,-1) → SLEventPostToPid(pid, event, attachAuthMessage: false)
  6. usleep(1_000)
  7. primerUp at (-1,-1) → SLEventPostToPid(pid, event, attachAuthMessage: false)
  8. usleep(100_000)  — Chromium needs gap to treat primer + target as separate gestures
  9. targetDown at real screen coords → SLEventPostToPid(pid, event, attachAuthMessage: false)
  10. usleep(1_000)
  11. targetUp at real screen coords → SLEventPostToPid(pid, event, attachAuthMessage: false)

(d) The double-post path in postBoth() (SkyLight + CGEvent.postToPid in sequence) is a belt-and-suspenders approach for the non-auth-signed path (right-clicks, triple-clicks, modifier-held clicks). Both posts are cursor-neutral.

LAYER 4 — AX PATH (UNCHANGED): Correct as stated.

SELF-INJECTED EVENT FILTERING (UNCHANGED): Correct as stated.

MACROS 14 ACTIVATION CHANGE (UNCHANGED): Correct — NSApplication.activate deprecation does not affect SkyLight-layer PSN calls.

KEY ADDITIONAL RISK NOT IN PROPOSAL:
macOS 26 Tahoe (current as of June 2026): yabai's space-switching commands are broken in 26.2+ beta, and the scripting-addition path (which yabai requires for Space operations) needs re-signing after each macOS update. The pure input-delivery path (SLPSPostEventRecordTo + SLEventPostToPid, which do not use the scripting addition) appears unaffected by reported breakage, but no confirmed verification exists yet on Tahoe for per-window background input. The dlopen/dlsym runtime resolution pattern means the code degrades gracefully on symbol removal rather than refusing to compile. Treat macOS 26 compatibility as requiring a validation spike on Tahoe hardware before GA.

BUFFER LAYOUT CAUTION:
Do NOT add `memset(buf + 0x20, 0xff, 0x10)` — this is present in yabai's make_key_window (a separate function from focus_without_raise) and causes SIGABRT on macOS 14.2.1 via CGSEncodeEventRecord/NSKeyedArchiver (confirmed by paneru issue #123). The proposed solution's buffer only sets 0x04, 0x08, 0x3C–0x3F, and 0x8A, which is safe.
risks.confidence: high — All major claims were verified against primary source files (trycua/cua-driver Swift source, koekeishiya/yabai C source, cua-driver GitHub issues #1503 and #123 in paneru). The two critical holes (macOS 14 keyboard gap, yabai SetFrontProcessWithOptions misattribution) are confirmed by reading actual code. The windowNumber: 0 error is confirmed by MouseInput.swift lines 662–667 and 282–284. The paneru crash confirms the make_key_window 0xFF-fill hazard. Tahoe breakage is confirmed by yabai issues #2656 and #2707 but limited to space-switching, not per-window input delivery.

holes: HOLE 1 — NSScreen.frame vs kCGWindowBounds coordinate-space mismatch in backingScale: NSScreen.frame uses Cocoa/AppKit bottom-left origin; kCGWindowBounds uses CG/AX top-left origin. Directly intersecting screen.frame (bottom-left) with a CGRect built from kCGWindowBounds coordinates (top-left) gives wrong results for any secondary monitor with non-zero y-offset. On a single-screen setup the primary screen always has origin (0,0) in both spaces, so the bug is latent. On a typical two-monitor setup (e.g., laptop built-in Retina left + external non-Retina right) the screen frames are placed at positive y in Cocoa space but positive y means down in CG space — the intersection area test will mis-identify which screen the window is on, and therefore return the wrong backingScaleFactor. The trycua/cua WindowCoordinateSpace.swift inherits this same issue (same source code, same pattern); its apparent production success is explained by primarily single-screen or symmetric-scale testing. FIX: before calling screen.frame.intersection(), convert the kCGWindowBounds rect to Cocoa/NSScreen space first — flip y using primaryScreenHeight - bounds.y - bounds.height — then intersect against screen.frame. OR use NSScreen.convertRectFromBacking / CGDisplayBounds (which is already in CG space like kCGWindowBounds), cross-reference by display ID via CGGetDisplaysWithRect, and read backingScaleFactor from that NSScreen. The proposed solution's comment 'screen.frame is in NSWindow/Cocoa bottom-left space, but intersection math works regardless because we only use the area, not the origin' is WRONG — the area computed is correct only when x,y origins happen to align, which they don't on secondary monitors.

HOLE 2 — kAXWindowMovedNotification fires at END of move, not continuously during live drag. Apple's own documentation for kAXWindowMovedNotification explicitly states: 'sent at the end of the window-move operation, not during it.' The proposed solution claims the AX notification 'fires within milliseconds of a window move' and provides geometry sync 'within one frame via AX notification'. For a window being actively dragged across the screen, the geometry metadata channel will be stale for the entire duration of the drag (which can be seconds), not milliseconds. During a drag, every click sent by the client will land at the pre-drag position. FIX: supplement AX notifications with a polling fallback during active drags. A CGEventTap observing .leftMouseDragged can detect when the user is dragging, and during that interval re-query CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo at ~30 Hz. Alternatively, observe NSWindowDidMoveNotification on the host for windows owned by the host process, or use SCStreamFrameInfo.contentRect (despite its documentation gap) as a per-frame geometry signal during active capture.

HOLE 3 — CGEvent.post(tap: .cghidEventTap) warps the cursor globally. The proposed test harness uses down.post(tap: .cghidEventTap). This tap location injects into the global HID stream — it physically moves the cursor on screen and is visible to the local user, which defeats the 'remote session invisibility' requirement of the product. trycua/cua's production code deliberately avoids this: it uses SLEventPostToPid (SkyLight private SPI) + CGEvent.postToPid to deliver to a specific PID without cursor movement. The proposed solution's injectClick function using .cghidEventTap is wrong for this architecture. FIX: use CGEvent.postToPid(targetPid) or the SkyLight SLEventPostToPid path for delivery. For the common case (non-Chromium AppKit windows), postToPid is sufficient and does not move the cursor. The proposed solution only needs Post Event privilege (CGPreflightPostEventAccess / CGRequestPostEventAccess), not the global tap path.

HOLE 4 — CGEvent injection via .cghidEventTap requires 'Post Event' privilege, not just 'Accessibility'. Apple's DTS engineer confirmed this in a developer forum thread: the correct pre-flight is CGPreflightPostEventAccess() and request is CGRequestPostEventAccess(). The proposed solution and test harness call down.post(tap: .cghidEventTap) but never mention the Post Event TCC entitlement. If the app only has Accessibility (for AX notifications) but not Post Event, the injection silently does nothing. This is distinct from the Accessibility permission. Developer ID non-sandboxed apps need both permissions listed separately.

HOLE 5 — SCStreamFrameInfo.scaleFactor is broken/undocumented: a confirmed Apple developer forum thread (thread/725283, post dated Jun '25) shows that SCStreamFrameInfo.scaleFactor.rawValue returns the string 'SCStreamUpdateFrameDisplayResolution' rather than a numeric value, meaning the documented property is non-functional. The proposed solution correctly identifies this as an issue under 'residualUncertainty', but the alternative solution 2 (using contentRect from CMSampleBuffer) remains viable only if you use the undocumented 'SCStreamUpdateFrameDisplayResolution' string key directly from the attachment dict. This is not a blocker for the recommended solution 1, but it is a confirmed trap for anyone implementing solution 2.

HOLE 6 — NSWindowDidChangeBackingPropertiesNotification, not kAXWindowMovedNotification, is the correct signal for scale-factor changes during cross-screen drags. The proposed solution uses AX window-move notifications to trigger backingScale re-queries. But AX notifications require the target app's window to be accessible and only fire at move-end. The correct cross-screen drag signal is NSWindowDidChangeBackingPropertiesNotification (for windows owned by the host process) or a polling approach for external windows. For the specific case of the captured window not being owned by the server process, a hybrid of AX notification (for end-of-move position) plus a CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo poll on each encoded frame covers both scenarios.

HOLE 7 — CGEvent tap silent-disable after re-signing. A documented macOS quirk (reported Feb 2026): after re-signing an app and launching via Finder/Dock (Launch Services path), CGEvent taps become functionally inert even though CGEvent.tapCreate() returns non-nil. The tap appears installed but callbacks never fire. This is not a concern for the proposed architecture (which doesn't rely on a tap for injection — only optionally for the test harness), but the test harness that uses CGEvent.tapCreate(.listenOnly) to verify injection coordinates will silently produce no results in this scenario. Fix for test harness: always check CGEvent.tapIsEnabled() after tapCreate and after any re-sign/re-launch.

holes: macOS feedData bypasses Ghostty.Surface.feedData entirely: GhosttyTerminalView.feedData() (line 705) calls ghostty_surface_feed_data directly without going through the Ghostty.Surface Swift wrapper. The @MainActor guarantee comes from GhosttyTerminalView's class-level isolation, not from the Ghostty.Surface.feedData @MainActor annotation. This distinction matters when building your own fork: Swift's @MainActor enforcement is on the Swift calling class, not on the C symbol itself. If you call ghostty_surface_feed_data from a non-Swift context or from a C-level callback, there is zero compile-time enforcement.

Ghostty's Metal renderer has its own internal CVDisplayLink (window-vsync = true by default, renderer/generic.zig:692-698). This link fires displayLinkCallback → draw_now.notify() → wakes renderer thread → drawFrame(false). The proposed solution implies calling ghostty_surface_draw from a background thread causes a data race on draw_mutex; in fact draw_mutex serializes concurrent callers correctly — the crash risk would only occur if you called drawFrame without holding draw_mutex, which the API does not expose directly. The practical implication for your design: because Ghostty already has vsync-driven autonomous draws when focused, your VVTerm-style CVDisplayLink driving ghostty_surface_refresh+draw is supplementary — needed only during idle periods (when Ghostty's internal link pauses) or for force-flush after feed_data. This is exactly what VVTerm implements (100ms idle timeout, display link stops when not rendering).

The proposed actor TerminalBridge code example has a subtle concurrency bug: flush() is annotated @MainActor but contains await self.drainPending(), which suspends and hops to the actor's executor. After the await resumes, execution is on the actor's executor — NOT guaranteed to be on MainActor. The subsequent ghostty_surface_feed_data call may therefore run off the main thread. Fix: make drainPending() synchronous (not async) and capture the [Data] array before the @MainActor context ends, OR use a single Task { @MainActor in ... } that does both the drain and the feed atomically without suspension.

ghostty_surface_refresh thread-safety: the proposed solution claims refresh touches core_surface state that is not thread-safe. This is incorrect. The actual call chain is refreshCallback() → queueRender() → renderer_thread.wakeup.notify(), where wakeup is an xev.Async designed to be notified from any thread (no mutex required). The main-thread requirement for this call in VVTerm comes solely from GhosttyTerminalView's @MainActor class isolation, not from internal threading concerns. Calling renderer_thread.wakeup.notify() from a background thread is technically safe at the Zig level; the Swift constraint is the enforcement layer.

CVDisplayLink callback threading: the callback fires on a dedicated HIGH-PRIORITY background thread, not the main thread. VVTerm correctly handles this with DispatchQueue.main.async { view.displayLinkTick() } inside the callback. The necessity of this hop is not just convention — it is required because displayLinkTick accesses @MainActor-isolated properties (needsRender, surface, isShuttingDown) of GhosttyTerminalView. The proposed solution mentions the hop but understates why it is required.

VVTerm's idle check timer runs on the .main DispatchQueue (not a background queue): setupIdleCheckTimer() uses DispatchSource.makeTimerSource(queue: .main). If your TCP project uses a background-thread timer for idle detection, you must explicitly dispatch back to main before accessing CVDisplayLinkIsRunning or needsRender.

ghostty_surface_feed_data does not exist in upstream Ghostty (confirmed: zero matches in upstream src/). The proposed solution correctly states this, but any fork implementing this symbol must export it from the Zig embedded apprt surface — likely as a thin wrapper that calls a method on the core_surface to inject data into the VT parser. VVTerm's implementation is proprietary. This means builders of custom forks must design their own feed_data path; the threading contract they choose will determine whether main-thread enforcement is compile-time (Swift @MainActor) or convention-only.

holes: Off-screen/minimized window frame starvation: Apple forum thread/720228 confirms that when showsCursor=false AND the captured window is off-screen or minimized, SCK delivers frames only on mouse movement. The proposed solution's primary use-case (an active on-screen terminal/coding window) is not affected, but if the user moves or minimizes the target window, the stream starves. This is distinct from cursor leakage but is a real behavioral gap not mentioned anywhere in the solution text.

Measurement harness uses Finder window but drives cursor with CGEventPost — no flaw in isolation, but the harness should be run with the window fully on-screen and in the foreground. If for any reason the Finder window used is off-screen or minimized during the test, showsCursor=false will cause frame starvation and the harness will produce zero frames, giving an inconclusive result rather than a clean pass/fail. The harness description does not guard against this.

showMouseClicks macOS 15+ interaction: The SDK header (line 257-259) explicitly states showMouseClicks 'will not be affected by showsCursor'. If showMouseClicks is ever set to true (not the default), click ripple circles will appear in frames even with showsCursor=false. The solution does not mention this. For the stated use-case (never setting showMouseClicks=true) this is not a problem, but the omission means a future developer could accidentally enable it and introduce visual artifacts.

SDK header lines cited as '252-254' in the solution are correct in content but the exact line numbers differ across SDK versions — in the macOS 26.5 SDK the showsCursor property is at line 252-254 (confirmed). The claim holds.

The OBS PR cited as showing 'no special-casing' correctly represents the code (setShowsCursor:!sc->hide_cursor at line 202, after all filter types are constructed). However the OBS update path (lines 428-446) has a logic inversion: the early-return condition checks 'sc->hide_cursor != show_cursor' which returns early when the cursor setting HAS changed, preventing the stream rebuild. This is an OBS-specific bug, not a SCK behavioral issue, and does not affect the proposed solution.

No Apple-published documentation explicitly states showsCursor=false works for SCContentFilter(desktopIndependentWindow:) specifically. All evidence is indirect (SDK header no-caveat + OBS uniform call + no reported leaks + no cursor keys in SCStreamFrameInfo). The residual empirical risk remains non-zero. The solution correctly classifies this as empirical and provides a measurement harness — verdict is BOUNDED not SOLVED.
risks.correctedSolution:
The core solution is correct and holds. Set `config.showsCursor = false` on `SCStreamConfiguration` paired with `SCContentFilter(desktopIndependentWindow: window)`. Primary sources confirm: (a) the SDK header (macOS 26.5 SDK SCStream.h line 252-254) declares `showsCursor` as a generic `SCStreamProperty` with no per-filter caveat; (b) the complete `SCStreamFrameInfo` key set in the same header (lines 390-444) contains no cursor position, cursor image, or cursor rect keys — cursor data is composited into the IOSurface by SCK or omitted entirely, with no partial-leak channel; (c) OBS Studio mac-sck-video-capture.m calls `setShowsCursor:` once after all three filter types are constructed, with no per-filter branching; (d) screencapturekit-rs FrameInfo struct independently confirms no cursor fields in CMSampleBuffer attachments. The fallback (cursor side-channel via CGEventTap / NSEvent global monitor) is architecturally already required for the Phase 2 GUI path and costs nothing incremental.

Three corrections to the solution text:

1. ADD: Off-screen/minimized window frame starvation. When showsCursor=false and the captured window goes off-screen or is minimized, SCK stops delivering frames (confirmed: Apple forum thread/720228 — frames arrive only on mouse movement in that scenario). For the primary use-case (active on-screen terminal window) this is irrelevant. However, if the remote desktop use-case ever needs to capture a minimized or off-screen window, a different strategy is needed: either set showsCursor=true (accept cursor in frame and strip client-side using the known cursor position from the side-channel) or keep showsCursor=false and accept that frame delivery stalls when the window is hidden. Document this in the implementation.

2. CORRECT the measurement harness: Add an explicit precondition that the test window (e.g., Finder) must be fully on-screen and in the foreground during the test. If the window is off-screen, showsCursor=false causes frame starvation and the harness produces zero buffers — an inconclusive result, not a pass. The current harness description does not guard against this.

3. ADD: showMouseClicks macOS 15+ note. The SDK header (line 257-259) states showMouseClicks 'will not be affected by showsCursor'. The default is NO (false), so this is safe for the stated use-case. But explicitly set `config.showMouseClicks = false` (or leave it at the default) and never set it to true if cursor-clean frames are required, because click ripple effects would appear independently of showsCursor.

The verdict is BOUNDED (not SOLVED) because Apple has not documented per-filter behavior of showsCursor explicitly, and the measurement harness is the correct way to close the remaining empirical gap. The harness is sound for on-screen windows with the above correction applied.

holes: DataRateLimits Swift code is wrong: the snippet uses [12_000_000 / 4, 1.0] = [3_000_000 bytes, 1.0 s] which is a 24 Mbps hard cap, double the intended 12 Mbps. The correct value is [12_000_000 / 8, 1.0] = [1_500_000 bytes, 1.0 s] for a 1-second window, or [3_000_000, 2.0] for the 2-second window described in the text. The description text (bytes_per_sec * 2, 2.0) is correct; only the Swift code is wrong.

kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main_AutoLevel in Session A may be silently overridden by the low-latency encoder. The SDK header (VTCompressionProperties.h line 1194) states EnableLowLatencyRateControl 'enforces... Only High profiles' -- but no kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_High_AutoLevel constant exists in the SDK (only Main, Main10, Main42210 are exposed). FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c line 975 passes HEVC_Main_AutoLevel even in low-latency mode and does not crash, implying the encoder silently promotes to its internal High-equivalent. The workaround is to either omit kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ProfileLevel entirely in Session A (let the encoder auto-select) or explicitly set HEVC_Main_AutoLevel knowing the encoder may override it. The real bitstream profile should be verified at runtime via VTSessionCopySerializableProperties. This is not a blocker but the ProfileLevel argument in Session A's VTSessionSetProperties call is at best redundant and at worst confusingly mismatched with documentation.

SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel interaction is unmentioned. SDK line 1538 states this property 'must be disabled when low latency rate control is enabled.' Session A is fine because the solution does not set it, but if the caller's VTCompressionSession setup guide is used and SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel is later added for quality tuning, it will conflict with EnableLowLatencyRateControl. The corrected solution should call VTSessionSetProperty(sessionA, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel, kVTQPModulationLevel_Disable) or document the incompatibility.

The Sidecar inference is speculative. The claim 'This is the architecture used in Apple's own Sidecar (inferred from their SCContentFilter + encoder design patterns)' has no primary source. Sidecar uses a private framework and its internal encoder architecture is not publicly documented. This should be removed or qualified as speculation, as it gives unwarranted authority to the two-session design choice.

kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Quality is described in VTCompressionProperties.h (lines 251-261) and the iOS 26.5 SDK forum thread confirms it works for HEVC on Apple Silicon, but the SDK comment says '1.0 implies lossless compression for encoders that support it' -- HEVC in VT on Apple Silicon does support it (confirmed via forums.developer.apple.com/thread/799316), but on iOS the Quality property may produce a different result than on macOS Apple Silicon hardware. Since this project targets macOS 14+ Apple Silicon hosts only, this is not a blocker for Session B.
risks.correctedSolution:
The two-session architecture is correct. All major architectural claims hold. The changes needed are:

(1) Fix the DataRateLimits Swift code. Replace:
  kVTCompressionPropertyKey_DataRateLimits: [12_000_000 / 4, 1.0]
with:
  kVTCompressionPropertyKey_DataRateLimits: [12_000_000 / 8, 1.0]
(= 1,500,000 bytes per 1-second window = 12 Mbps hard cap matching AverageBitRate)

(2) For Session A's ProfileLevel: either omit kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ProfileLevel entirely in the encoderSpec (safest -- let the low-latency encoder auto-select its required High-equivalent profile), or keep HEVC_Main_AutoLevel knowing it will be silently overridden. The "Only High profiles" constraint from the SDK header means the encoder will enforce a High-tier bitstream regardless of what you pass for HEVC; no HEVC High profile constant is exposed, so the encoder handles this internally. Recommended: omit ProfileLevel from Session A entirely.

(3) Add explicit SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel disable for Session A:
  kVTCompressionPropertyKey_SpatialAdaptiveQPLevel: kVTQPModulationLevel_Disable
(SDK line 1538 mandates this when EnableLowLatencyRateControl is true; macOS 15+ only -- wrap in availability check, ignore error on macOS 14)

(4) Remove the Sidecar inference claim -- it has no primary source.

Everything else in the solution is verified correct against the iOS 26.5 SDK headers and FFmpeg source:
- EnableLowLatencyRateControl in encoderSpec dict at create time: confirmed (SDK line 1202, FFmpeg line 1722-1724)
- AverageBitRate as SInt32: confirmed (SDK line 213)
- MaxAllowedFrameQP available macOS 12+: confirmed (SDK line 1214) -- project targets macOS 14+, no issue

- Quality+ABR are mutually exclusive by design in FFmpeg (lines 1250-1283); the two-session architecture avoids the ambiguity entirely
- HEVC low-latency is ARM64-only, requires explicit bitrate (FFmpeg lines 1714-1725): confirmed
- RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder in encoderSpec at create time: valid on macOS 10.9+ (SDK line 807)
- ForceKeyFrame per Session B encode call: confirmed (SDK line 876)
- AllowTemporalCompression=false produces all-intra keyframe-only output: confirmed (SDK line 164-170)

Residual empirical items (not blockers):
- MaxAllowedFrameQP=40 as text legibility floor: still empirical. Apple Silicon HEVC QP range is 0-51; QP 40 is a reasonable starting point based on general HEVC perceptual curves but the actual cliff on Apple's encoder may differ ±3 steps. One-time visual inspection on target hardware remains advised.
- Session B lossless HEVC size at 1440p: 4-8x Session A frame size estimate is plausible but should be confirmed for the specific SCContentFilter window size in use; network budget for upgrade bursts must be validated against the WireGuard/NetBird path MTU and congestion window.

holes: CRITICAL API VERSION GATE — The proposed session creation code uses kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder (and its Enable/Using counterparts) without any version check. All three keys are API_AVAILABLE(macos(10.9), ios(17.0)). On iOS 16 and earlier, the CFStringRef constants are undefined at link time (using them produces a NULL key in the dict, which is silently ignored). Session creation will succeed without error but will not require or guarantee hardware decode. An iPhone 11 / iPhone 12 on iOS 16 is a plausible client device. Fix: either set minimum iOS deployment target to 17.0, or add @available(iOS 17, *) guards and fall back to EnableHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder without the Require semantics, then query kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder after session creation to confirm HW decode is active.

HARDWARE KEY AVAILABILITY ASYMMETRY — kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_EnableHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder is also ios(17.0)+. On macOS 10.15+ hardware decode is on by default. But on iOS 16 and earlier there is no programmatic way to require HW decode via the session spec dictionary. The post-session query kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder is also ios(17.0)+. Below iOS 17 you cannot query whether HW decode is active. The solution's decode-time measurement harness is the only remaining signal for iOS 16 clients.

ISSUE #1885 'EXTRA FRAME' CLAIM IS UNQUANTIFIED — The proposed solution states AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer adds 'at least one extra frame (~33-41 ms at 24-30 fps)'. The actual issue #1885 contains only qualitative user perception: 'cursor movement consistently behind, as if the display path is buffering one or more extra frames'. There are no measured millisecond latency numbers in the issue. The 33-41 ms figure is the solution author's inference. The claim is directionally correct but the specific quantification is not sourced.

ISSUE #1087 LATENCY NUMBERS ARE ESTIMATES — The '~4-6 ms' M2 Mac decode time and '~1 ms' M2 iPad decode time are anecdotal user reports, not systematic measurements. The iPad figure is explicitly a guess ('I would assume ~1ms'). The expected value range in the measurement harness (2-8 ms median, p99 < 16 ms for M-series at 1080p30) is presented as authoritative but is derived from a single anecdote on one resolution. p99 and thermal-throttle behavior remain unmeasured.

iOS BACKGROUND TRANSITION HANG WITH SYNCHRONOUS DECODE — Apple Developer Forum thread/19046 and rdar://25828950 document that VTDecompressionSessionDecodeFrame with synchronous flags (decodeFlags=0) can hang indefinitely on iOS when the app transitions to the background. Apple confirmed this is a bug. The proposed solution does not mention this risk or a mitigation. Mitigation: the decode loop must observe UIApplicationDidEnterBackgroundNotification and stop calling DecodeFrame before the background transition completes; or use kVTDecodeFrame_EnableAsynchronousDecompression with a timeout-guarded WaitForAsynchronousFrames so the decode thread is not permanently blocked.

MEASUREMENT HARNESS SCOPE IS DECODE-ONLY — The harness measures t_submit to callback_fire, which is the VTDecompressionSession decode latency. It does not measure the remaining pipeline: CVPixelBuffer → CVMetalTextureCacheCreateTextureFromImage → CAMetalLayer.nextDrawable (can block 5-13 ms under GPU pressure per Apple Dev Forum thread/722434) → Metal render encoder → commandBuffer.commit → displayScanout. The decision rule ('if p99 < one frame period: PASS') applies only to the decode stage; a passing decode measurement does not prove the full motion-to-photon budget is met. The harness should be extended to also timestamp the CAMetalLayer presentDrawable completion.

FFmpeg PATTERN INCLUDES WaitForAsynchronousFrames AFTER decodeFlags=0 — The FFmpeg videotoolbox.c path passes decodeFlags=0 but immediately follows with VTDecompressionSessionWaitForAsynchronousFrames. This is a defensive belt-and-suspenders pattern; with decodeFlags=0 the wait is a no-op. However it signals that the maintainers do not fully trust the synchronous contract alone. The proposed solution correctly identifies decodeFlags=0 as sufficient per spec but should note that WaitForAsynchronousFrames is harmless defensive practice used by FFmpeg.

macOS 26 REGRESSION (issue #1696) — One user reports 80ms decode latency on macOS 26 'Tahoe' with Intel GPU. While this machine has a dGPU (not Apple Silicon), it shows that OS-level VT changes can spike decode latency. The decision rule in the harness should be tested on the target macOS version, not just assumed stable across OS upgrades. Re-run the harness on each major macOS release.

risks.correctedSolution:
The core solution holds: use kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=kCFBooleanTrue as the primary hardware gate; VTCompressionSessionCreate returns non-zero immediately when hardware is unavailable; surface the error to the user at window-open time. The following corrections and additions are required.

**Correction 1 — Low-latency mode is ARM64-only for HEVC (important for future roadmap)**

EnableLowLatencyRateControl works with HEVC only on ARM64 (Apple Silicon). On Intel Macs it is H.264 only (returns -12902 for HEVC). Since the project targets macOS 14+ Apple Silicon exclusively, HEVC + low-latency is available. If the design later combines RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true and EnableLowLatencyRateControl for HEVC, both must be in the encoderSpec; if hardware is present and supporting it, this works on Apple Silicon (confirmed by FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c line 1714-1724 and WWDC21). Error -12902 in the Require+LowLatency combination on Apple Silicon indicates a hardware configuration issue or older firmware, not a fundamental limitation.

**Correction 2 — UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder query: use once at session creation, never poll**

The post-creation query is useful for logging but unreliable as a runtime signal. Query it exactly once after VTCompressionSessionCreate succeeds. Never call VTSessionCopyProperty or VTSessionSetProperty on a high-frequency dispatch queue — the documented race condition (kVTVideoEncoderMalfunctionErr, mediaserverd crash) occurs when these are called frequently without delay. The correct one-time query pattern from the solution is sound; the hazard is only if the query is later moved to a per-frame or per-second polling loop.

**Correction 3 — Window resize must invalidate and recreate the session**

Width and height are fixed at VTCompressionSessionCreate time. When a captured window is resized by the user, the session must be invalidated and a new session created at the new dimensions. The WindowEncoderPool must add a resize path:

```swift
func resizeWindow(_ id: WindowID, width: Int, height: Int) throws {
    closeWindow(id)           // invalidates old session, frees hardware slot
    try openWindow(id, width: width, height: height)  // acquires new slot
}
```

This resize path temporarily consumes two hardware encoder slots if the old session is not invalidated before the new one is created — closeWindow first is mandatory.

**Correction 4 — Chip table corrected**

Correct table as of June 2026 (M4 Ultra is not announced; M3 Ultra is the current Ultra-tier):

| Chip tier | Encode engines | Expected HW session ceiling* |
|---|---|---|
| M1/M2/M3/M4 base | 1 | ~2–4 sessions at 1080p–1440p |
| M1/M2/M3/M4 Pro | 1 | ~2–4 sessions at 1080p–1440p |
| M1/M2/M3/M4 Max | 2 | ~4–8 sessions |
| M1/M2 Ultra | 4 | ~8–16 sessions |
| M3 Ultra | 4 (confirmed, Apple press release March 2025) | ~8–16 sessions |

*M4 Ultra not yet announced. Counts from Apple tech specs pages and Softron documentation.

**Correction 5 — VTEncoderXPCService race condition: characterize accurately**

The race is real (confirmed on M3 Max in April 2025, error -12905). The warm-up session pattern is reasonable engineering practice but Apple has not confirmed it as the fix. The project should implement the warm-up session, but also implement retry logic: if VTCompressionSessionCreate with Require=true fails on the first attempt with -12905, retry once after 50–100ms before surfacing an error to the user. Apple's root cause is unknown; the retry + warm-up combination is the best available defense.

**Correction 6 — Output pipeline: multiple NAL units per CMSampleBuffer on macOS 26+**

On macOS 26 (iOS 26) the HEVC encoder outputs multiple NAL units per compressed CMSampleBuffer. Any NAL-unit consumer (RTP packetizer, H.265 bytestream framer) must iterate all NAL units in the sample, not assume one. Detect by scanning NAL unit type values 16–21 for IDR frames rather than relying solely on AV_PKT_FLAG_KEY.

**What is unchanged and correct**
- RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true as the primary gate: confirmed correct.
- Fail-fast at window-open time: correct and preferable to silent CPU fallback.
- kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr (-12900) in low-latency mode means the session is hardware-accelerated (WWDC21 explicitly states this).
- The measurement harness (Steps 1–4) correctly isolates the variable: create sessions one at a time with Require=true, observe the N at which creation fails. The harness correctly adds a short sleep between creations to let the XPC service settle.
- Product implication: 1–4 simultaneous remote GUI windows on single-engine Apple Silicon (base/Pro) is comfortably within the hardware budget at 1080p; the risk is real only at higher window counts or 4K resolutions.

holes: CRITICAL — CryptoHandler nonce state must be preserved across reconnects, not re-created: ET's BackedWriter and BackedReader each hold a CryptoHandler whose incrementing nonce encodes the count of all messages ever sent on that session since initial connect(). During recover(), ET reuses the same CryptoHandler instance; the replay buffer already holds pre-encrypted packets stamped with the correct advancing nonces, and the remote side's CryptoHandler has been decrypting them with its own synchronized counter. If the Swift port constructs a fresh CryptoHandler (nonce reset to zero) on reconnect while the remote side's counter is at N, every decrypt call will fail with an authentication error. The solution never mentions this: a Swift reimplementation must persist nonce state across reconnects. However, if the system uses raw TCP (no application-layer encryption — as stated: 'WireGuard does ChaCha20-Poly1305 ... No app-layer crypto'), then no CryptoHandler is needed at all and this hole does not apply. The solution conflates the ET protocol's crypto machinery with the plain-TCP design being built; implementors must be explicit that if they drop ET's CryptoHandler they must also drop the nonce-sequenced backup buffer format and replace it with a plain-bytes sequence-numbered buffer.

PROTOCOL BUG — SequenceHeader field type is int32, not int64: The proto file (proto/ET.proto line 30) defines 'optional int32 sequenceNumber = 1'. BackedWriter::sequenceNumber and BackedReader::sequenceNumber are int64 internally (BackedWriter.hpp line 102, BackedReader.hpp line 81). The proto truncates to 32 bits at the wire. For a 64 MB buffer of small PTY packets (~100 bytes each), the buffer holds ~640K packets, well within int32 (2 billion). For a continuous long-running session with many reconnects over weeks, int32 could theoretically wrap. The solution does not mention this, but it is not a near-term blocker for the described use-case.

HANDSHAKE ORDERING DISCREPANCY — The solution's handshake diagram shows the client sending the RETURNING_CLIENT intent, but the actual ET protocol shows that it is the *server* that decides and sends ConnectResponse(RETURNING_CLIENT) based on matching the clientId. The client sends only ConnectRequest(clientId, version) and then immediately calls recover() — which writes SequenceHeader first, then reads the remote SequenceHeader, then writes CatchupBuffer, then reads CatchupBuffer (Connection.cpp lines 104-133). The server (ServerConnection.cpp line 119) sends RETURNING_CLIENT then calls recoverClient() which also runs recover() — both sides enter the same symmetric recover() function concurrently. The solution's diagram has an extra explicit 'RETURNING_CLIENT intent' step on the client side that does not exist in the code; the solution is slightly wrong but this does not break correctness if the implementor reads the actual code.

BLINK MISATTRIBUTION — The solution's code snippet in Phase A credits Blink's BackgroundTaskMonitor with calling stopPTYOutput() in the expiration handler. The actual Blink source (BackgroundTaskMonitor.swift lines 85-87) only calls self.stop() — a callback the user supplies — and the only instantiation in the codebase (WebSocketServer.swift line 55) passes a stop callback that cancels an NWListener, not a PTY reader. Blink is a reference terminal, not a PTY host; stopPTYOutput() does not exist in Blink. The conceptual approach (stop work in expiration handler) is correct, but the attributed code pattern is fabricated. The corrected pattern is: pass a 'stop' closure that pauses PTY drain, matching the BackgroundTaskMonitor contract.

SCENEPHASE LIMITATION — The solution presents SwiftUI @Environment(\.scenePhase) and UIApplication.willResignActiveNotification as equivalent alternatives ('or'). They are not equivalent for this use-case. SwiftUI scenePhase delivers only 3 states (active, inactive, background) with no 'will' event granularity, and is documented to have bugs in multi-scene apps (Jesse Squires, 2024-06-29). For a terminal that must gate socket closure precisely before suspension, UIApplicationDelegate willResignActive / didEnterBackground notifications are the correct choice; scenePhase introduces an extra delay because SwiftUI posts .background after the UIKit notification, not before.

SOCKET ERROR CODE NUANCE — TN2277 says the error is 'intentionally unspecified' and 'in many cases will be EBADF'. Forum thread 757385 (2024 MQTT discussion) updates this: in recent OS versions (iOS 14+) the error is ECONNABORTED for NWConnection, not EBADF. EBADF is a legacy BSD socket artifact. The solution correctly mentions both but presents them as equivalent alternatives; implementors should expect NWError.posix(.ECONNABORTED) from NWConnection.stateUpdateHandler on wake, and ENODATA for graceful close (EOF). The solution's mention of ENODATA as one of the error codes is correct per Quinn's 2019 forum post (thread 113809).

NETBIRD NETWORK EXTENSION SUSPENSION RISK IS CONFIRMED BUT UNDERSTATED — The solution's residual uncertainty #2 notes that 'the iOS VPN extension may also be suspended.' The NetBird iOS client (PacketTunnelProvider.swift, netbirdio/ios-client) uses NEPacketTunnelProvider, which runs as a system extension separate from the app process. NEPacketTunnelProvider is NOT subject to app suspension — it is managed by the OS and persists even when the main app is killed. However, the extension does respond to network changes (NWPathMonitor callbacks) and restarts itself on network type change or recovery from no-network (handleNetworkChange -> restartClient). This means the WireGuard/NetBird interface *will* survive app suspension, but the interface address may cycle briefly during the extension restart on wake. The ~1-second debounce in restartClient() means the host IP is stable. The proposed NWConnection .waiting state will cover the brief interface restart. This is less risky than the solution implies; the residual uncertainty should be classified as low risk.

BUFFERED_ONLY vs SKIPPED DISTINCTION MISSED — The solution says SKIPPED is returned 'when PTY output would exceed the offline gate.' The actual BackedWriter.cpp (line 19) shows SKIPPED is returned only when socketFd < 0 AND backupSize + packet.length() > DISCONNECT_BUFFER_BYTES. When socketFd < 0 AND within the 4MB gate, the return is BUFFERED_ONLY (line 40), not SKIPPED. BUFFERED_ONLY means the packet was encrypted and added to the backup buffer successfully. The caller must distinguish: SKIPPED = gate exceeded, stop draining PTY; BUFFERED_ONLY = still within gate, continue. The solution conflates SKIPPED with all offline buffering, which would cause premature PTY pause if implemented literally.
risks.correctedSolution:
**The core architecture is sound and should be implemented as proposed with the following corrections:**

---

**Correction 1 — Crypto layer: explicitly out-of-scope for this system**

ET's CryptoHandler uses libsodium secretbox with an incrementing per-session nonce. This nonce *must* be preserved across reconnects (not reset), because the replay buffer contains pre-encrypted messages with already-consumed nonce values. Since this system uses plain TCP over NetBird/WireGuard (ChaCha20-Poly1305 at the tunnel level, no app-layer crypto), the Swift implementation must NOT port CryptoHandler. The BackedWriter/BackedReader analogue stores raw bytes (plaintext), not libsodium-encrypted packets. Document this explicitly so future implementors do not accidentally add a nonce-resetting crypto layer.

---

**Correction 2 — SequenceHeader is int32 at the wire**

ET's proto defines `optional int32 sequenceNumber`. For long-running sessions, the internal counters are int64. If building a custom protocol (not proto2), use int64 / varint throughout to avoid silent truncation on sessions with billions of small packets.

---

**Correction 3 — Actual handshake sequence (server decides RETURNING_CLIENT)**

The correct sequence (verified against Connection.cpp:96-141 and ServerConnection.cpp:119-123):

```
Client                                    Host
──────                                    ────
ConnectRequest(clientId, version)  →
                                   ← ConnectResponse(RETURNING_CLIENT)  // host decides based on clientId lookup
SequenceHeader(reader.seqno)       →      // both sides call recover() concurrently from here
                                   ← SequenceHeader(reader.seqno)
CatchupBuffer(writer.recover(remoteSeq)) →
                                   ← CatchupBuffer(writer.recover(clientSeq))
reader.revive(fd, catchupFromHost)
writer.revive(fd)
                                          reader.revive(fd, catchupFromClient)
                                          writer.revive(fd)
```

The client does NOT send a separate "RETURNING_CLIENT intent". It sends ConnectRequest; the host inspects its session table and sets the response status. Both sides then enter the symmetric recover() call simultaneously.

---

**Correction 4 — Blink BackgroundTaskMonitor expiration handler**

The actual Blink source calls `self.stop()` (a user-supplied closure) then `endBackgroundTask`. The stop callback in Blink's only instantiation cancels an NWListener. For this system, the correct expiration-handler closure is:

```swift
backgroundTask = UIApplication.shared.beginBackgroundTask { [weak self] in
    self?.taskIsSuspended = true
    self?.pausePTYDrainLoop()      // stop reading from PTY master fd
    UIApplication.shared.endBackgroundTask(self!.backgroundTask)
    self?.backgroundTask = .invalid
}
```

`pausePTYDrainLoop()` stops the loop that calls `read()` on the PTY master fd. This lets the host BackedWriter accumulate up to 4 MB in its offline gate (BUFFERED_ONLY path), then returns SKIPPED when the gate is exceeded, which stalls PTY output via kernel backpressure — exactly as the solution describes, but the code shape should match Blink's contract.

---

**Correction 5 — Use UIKit lifecycle notifications, not SwiftUI scenePhase**

Use `UIApplication.didEnterBackgroundNotification` and `UIApplication.willEnterForegroundNotification` (UIKit, available since iOS 4). `SwiftUI @Environment(\.scenePhase)` is documented to have multi-scene bugs and delivers only 3 coarse states with no "will" granularity (confirmed by Jesse Squires, 2024). For a coding tool with a single scene, scenePhase may work, but UIKit notifications are more reliable and Blink itself uses them.

---

**Correction 6 — BUFFERED_ONLY vs SKIPPED are distinct return values**

BackedWriter.write() returns:
- `BUFFERED_ONLY` — no socket, within 4 MB gate: packet was encrypted+buffered, **continue draining PTY**.
- `SKIPPED` — no socket, gate exceeded: packet was dropped, **pause PTY drain loop**.
- `SUCCESS` — socket alive, sent: continue normally.
- `WROTE_WITH_FAILURE` — socket died mid-write: close socket, start reconnect thread.

The PTY read loop must check for SKIPPED specifically, not treat all non-SUCCESS as pause signals.

---

**Correction 7 — NetBird Network Extension survives app suspension (lower risk than stated)**

`NEPacketTunnelProvider` is a system extension process, not subject to app suspension. The host IP is stable. The extension does briefly restart when the network interface changes post-wake (1-second debounce in `restartClient()`), during which `NWConnection` will enter `.waiting` and auto-retry. This is already handled by the proposed NWConnection `stateUpdateHandler`. Classify residual uncertainty #2 as low risk, not unknown.

---

**Everything else in the proposed solution is verified correct:**
- `MAX_BACKUP_BYTES = 64 MB` and `DISCONNECT_BUFFER_BYTES = 4 MB` confirmed at BackedWriter.hpp:32-34.
- `beginBackgroundTask` ~30-second budget confirmed by Quinn (Apple DTS, forums/thread/85066); always assume it can be 0.
- NWConnection requires a pending `.receive()` to detect dead connections — confirmed by Quinn (forums/thread/113809, 2019).
- Always create a new `NWConnection` after `.failed`; use `restart()` only for `.waiting`. Confirmed.
- `recover()` throws `"Client is too far behind server."` when replay buffer is exhausted — confirmed at BackedWriter.cpp:106.
- TN2277 socket fate: error is unspecified; in practice ECONNABORTED on NWConnection in recent iOS (not EBADF). Both are handled by `.failed` state transition.
- `BackedReader.revive()` drains catchup buffer before resuming live reads — confirmed at BackedReader.hpp:55.
- `BackedWriter.revive()` only updates the socketFd — confirmed at BackedWriter.cpp:109.

holes: CRITICAL CODE BUG — isValidSignature is not a throwing function. The proposed Swift code uses `try key.isValidSignature(...)` which does not compile. Per the CryptoKit.framework swiftinterface (iOS 13.0 SDK, arm64-apple-ios): `public func isValidSignature<S, D>(_ signature: S, for data: D) -> Swift.Bool` — it returns Bool, not throws. The `try` keyword must be removed. The correct call is `let valid = key.isValidSignature(Data(signature), for: Data(nonce))` without try.

WRONG API CITED FOR KEY GENERATION — The solution says 'SecKeyCreateRandomKey with kSecAttrKeyTypeECSECPrimeRandom / 256-bit' to generate an Ed25519 keypair. This is wrong: kSecAttrKeyTypeECSECPrimeRandom generates ECDSA keys on the NIST P-256 (secp256r1) prime curve, not Ed25519/EdDSA on the Edwards curve. These are distinct algorithms. CryptoKit's Curve25519.Signing.PrivateKey() is the correct API (available macOS 10.15+, iOS 13.0+, confirmed from swiftinterface). The SecKeyCreateRandomKey mention is either a fallback thought or a hallucination of a merged concept — it must be removed from the solution.

SECURE ENCLAVE CLAIM IS WRONG FOR Curve25519 — The solution states 'keys can be stored in the Secure Enclave on modern Apple hardware'. This is false for Curve25519/Ed25519. The Secure Enclave only supports P-256 (secp256r1) via SecureEnclave.P256.Signing.PrivateKey. From the same swiftinterface: the SecureEnclave namespace exposes only P256.Signing and P256.KeyAgreement — there is no SecureEnclave.Curve25519. The correct statement is: if Secure Enclave backing is desired, the solution must switch to P-256 (SecureEnclave.P256.Signing) on both host and client, which changes the signing algorithm from Ed25519 to ECDSA-P256. The Keychain can store Curve25519 private keys with kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock protection but without hardware-backed extraction resistance.

NWListener INTERFACE-BINDING CLAIM NEEDS CLARIFICATION — The solution claims 'NWListener with requiredInterface set to the NetBird utun interface' as if NWInterface.InterfaceType has a VPN/utun value. The Network framework swiftinterface shows InterfaceType has only five cases: .other, .wifi, .cellular, .wiredEthernet, .loopback. The NetBird utun interface (utun10, etc.) would classify as .other. Using requiredInterfaceType = .other would affect ALL 'other' interfaces, not just NetBird. More practically, Apple dev forums report that requiredInterfaceType has no observable effect on NWListener binding for Bonjour/service advertisement (Quinn/Apple DTS confirmed this is unresolved). The correct, reliable mechanism for this goal is NWParameters.requiredLocalEndpoint = .hostPort(host: netbirdIP, port: ptyPort) — binding to the specific 100.x.x.x IP of the NetBird interface. This is confirmed to work for NWListener. Alternatively, POSIX bind() to the specific IP is completely reliable. The solution should be updated to remove the requiredInterfaceType claim and replace it with requiredLocalEndpoint.

NETBIRD USERSPACE FIREWALL SCOPE CONFIRMED BUT NUANCE MISSED — The solution correctly describes NetBird macOS ACL as userspace. Source confirmed: /client/firewall/create.go (build tag `!linux || android`) unconditionally selects uspfilter (userspace packet filter) because iface.IsUserspaceBind() is always true on macOS (/client/iface/iface_new.go sets userspaceBind: true). The solution's claim is accurate. However, the solution omits one additional risk: the uspfilter has an env var NB_ENABLE_LOCAL_FORWARDING that, if set, forwards loopback-bound traffic through the WireGuard tunnel — meaning a service bound to 127.0.0.1 could become reachable from the NetBird peer. The PTY daemon should NOT be bound to 0.0.0.0 or 127.0.0.1 even as a temporary state; it should bind only to the NetBird IP from the first listen() call.

MOSH ANALOGY IS SLIGHTLY INACCURATE — The solution says Mosh passes a 'one-time AES key via SSH'. The actual mechanism: the mosh-server process prints 'MOSH CONNECT <port> <key>' to stdout, which is captured by the mosh SSH session and passed as MOSH_KEY env var to the client. The key is a 128-bit AES key (22-byte base64), not HMAC-based, and it is transmitted over the existing SSH channel, not over the raw UDP transport. The ET analogy is also slightly off: ET sends a 16-char clientid + 32-char passkey via stdin to etterminal (which is piped over SSH), and this passkey seeds libsodium secretbox encryption on port 2022 — it is NOT Ed25519. These analogies correctly identify the pattern (bootstrap channel carries auth material) but the proposed pairing handshake (HMAC of a displayed token + Ed25519 for ongoing auth) is a stronger and different design than either reference. The analogies are broadly valid but should not be cited as 'the same mechanism'.

SETUP KEY RISK CONFIRMED — The cons entry for the NetBird-ACL-only approach correctly identifies the reusable setup key risk. This is confirmed by NetBird docs: setup keys auto-assign groups to enrolled peers, and a leaked reusable key can enroll a rogue device that inherits all group-based ACLs. The solution's App-layer handshake provides the correct backstop.
